intro: Baremetrics and PostHog both show up under the product analytics category, but they solve slightly different jobs. Baremetrics uses subscription from $129/mo with free tier, while PostHog runs on usage-based pricing with free tier. Lock-in is high for Baremetrics and low for PostHog. Transparency lands at 4/5 versus 5/5. Baremetrics fits teams working on understanding user behavior (funnels, retention), while PostHog is a closer match when the job is want analytics + feature flags + session replay in one. Worth noting: Baremetrics is explicitly not for simple projects; PostHog is explicitly not for enterprise needing dedicated support. The honest trade-off: Baremetrics trades off on pre-product stage; PostHog trades off on enterprise needing dedicated support — Amplitude is more enterprise. On the plus side, Baremetrics highlights understanding user behavior (funnels, retention), while PostHog points to analytics + feature flags + session replay in one. Baremetrics's documentation also calls out data-driven product decisions. PostHog similarly notes open-source with self-hosted option.
quick_take: Baremetrics is for understanding user behavior (funnels, retention); PostHog is for want analytics + feature flags +; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Baremetrics if your project is understanding user behavior (funnels, retention), a subscription starting at $129/mo fits your budget, you are willing to accept the high lock-in called out in our data.
verdict_b: Choose PostHog if your project is want analytics + feature flags + session replay in one, usage-based billing at Usage-based, price decreases at scale matches your volume, you want to keep future migration cheap.

faq.a: Baremetrics uses subscription from $129/mo with free tier, and PostHog uses usage-based pricing with free tier. The pricing models are different, so a direct cheaper-than comparison depends on your volume and usage pattern.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Baremetrics to PostHog?
faq.a: Our data puts Baremetrics at high lock-in (analytics derived from stripe), and PostHog at low lock-in (open-source, self-hosted, raw data access via data warehouse). Expect real migration work out of Baremetrics — plan for data export, re-integration, and downtime testing before cutting over.

PostHog scores 5/5 on developer experience in our data, while Baremetrics scores 3/5, so PostHog has the edge on docs and SDK quality by that measure. That gap is reinforced by transparency scores of 5/5 versus 4/5. Still, run a small integration spike on both before deciding — team familiarity with a given SDK style often matters more than a one-point score gap.

faq.q: Is PostHog a good alternative to Baremetrics?
faq.a: PostHog is a reasonable alternative to Baremetrics when your workload leans more toward want analytics + feature flags + session replay in one than understanding user behavior (funnels, retention). The pricing model shifts too — Baremetrics is subscription from $129/mo with free tier, PostHog is usage-based pricing with free tier — so expect the cost profile to change as well. One caveat: PostHog is explicitly not for enterprise needing dedicated support, so check that constraint against your use-case before switching.
